preamble: The shift from the alveolar stop [t] to the postalveolar fricative [ʃ] is a form of palatalization followed by deaffrication. It typically occurs when [t] is followed by a palatal glide [j] or high front vowel, forcing the tongue toward the palate. This process creates an affricate [tʃ] that later simplifies into a fricative.

examples.note: Georgian shows affricationand fricativization of dentals in palatal environments, contributing to harmonic clustering patterns.
Historical palatalization of Latin loanwords in English.
The palatalized cluster [kl] shifted to [tʃ] and eventually deaffricated to [ʃ].

sources: Baugh & Cable: A History of the English Language
